blogPosts.excerpt: You spent $300 on a curriculum and now your child dreads school. Here's how to diagnose what's wrong, when to switch, and how to find something better…
blogPosts.content:
# What to Do When Your Curriculum Isn't Working

It happens to nearly every homeschool family. You researched for weeks, spent real money, and started the year with genuine excitement — and now your child is miserable and school has become a daily battle.

First: this is not a failure. It is data.

---

## Step 1: Diagnose Before You Switch

**The curriculum doesn't match your child's learning style.** A kinesthetic child doing workbook-heavy curricula will resist. This is the most common reason curricula fail.

**The curriculum doesn't match your teaching style.** Working against your instincts every day is exhausting. Curriculum failure is sometimes parent burnout, not child resistance.

**The pace is wrong.** Too fast creates frustration. Too slow creates boredom. Both feel like failure but have different solutions.

**The subject itself is the problem.** Switching curricula won't fix a child's underlying resistance to a subject — though some programs are more engaging than others.

**You haven't given it enough time.** The first 4–6 weeks of any curriculum are rough. If you're in week 3, give it more time.

---

## Step 2: Know When It's Actually Time to Switch

- School consistently takes 2–3× longer than it should due to resistance
- Daily tears (yours or theirs) have become the norm for over 6 weeks
- Your child has stopped retaining material they previously mastered
- The teaching style required is one you genuinely cannot sustain

---

## Step 3: Recoup Your Investment First

**Sell what isn't working.** Homeschool Classifieds and local Facebook groups move curriculum quickly at 50–70 cents on the dollar.

**Try before you buy.** [Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html), [Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187), and [Simply Charlotte Mason](https://simplycharlottemason.com/ref/0v1y8/) all offer free sample downloads.

**Borrow first.** Ask your co-op if anyone has the curriculum you're considering and would let you borrow it for a week.

---

## Step 4: Use Our Tools

Use our [curriculum comparison tool](/) to filter by your child's grade, teaching style, budget, and any special needs. Take our [2-minute quiz](/) for personalized recommendations based on your specific situation.

---

## Step 5: Bridge Strategy While You Transition

**Math:** Khan Academy (free) works as a placeholder for any grade while you evaluate options.

**Reading:** [All About Reading](https://www.allaboutlearningpress.net/go.php?id=3107) is structured enough to continue regardless of what else shifts.

**Everything else:** Give yourself 4–6 weeks of informal learning — living books, documentaries, field trips, projects. This is not falling behind. It's a reset.

---

## Top Picks for Families Who've Had a Curriculum Fail

**[Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html)** — For children who shut down with worksheets but come alive with stories.

**[Simply Charlotte Mason](https://simplycharlottemason.com/ref/0v1y8/)** — For families overwhelmed by overscheduling who want a gentler approach.

**[Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187)** — For families wanting structure and faith integration at an accessible price.

**[Schoolhouse Teachers](https://schoolhouseteachers.com/ref/119831/)** — Hundreds of courses on one subscription — ideal for exploring what works before committing to a full purchase.

---

## The Most Important Thing

Switching curricula is not quitting. It is responsive parenting. The families who homeschool successfully for years are not the ones who chose perfectly the first time — they are the ones who paid attention, adjusted, and kept going.

---

# How to Homeschool When Both Parents Work

The most common objection from two-income families: *"We both work. There's no one home to teach."*

Here's what that misses: homeschooling does not require a parent present and teaching every hour of the school day. A well-designed homeschool for a working family might involve 1–2 hours of parent-directed learning in the evenings, and several hours of self-directed work during the day.

It looks different from traditional homeschooling. But it works.

---

## The Core Strategy: Maximize Independent Learning

The key is choosing curricula your child can do largely on their own, reserving your direct involvement for teaching new concepts and reviewing completed work. By 3rd or 4th grade, most children can work through online lessons, complete assignments, and self-check independently.

---

## Best Curricula for Working Families

### 1. Time4Learning — Best Overall for Self-Directed Learning
**Price:** ~$30–$55/month
**Grades:** Pre-K–12

Time4Learning is purpose-built for independent learning. Students log in, work through animated and interactive lessons at their own pace, get automatically graded — all without a parent present. The parent dashboard shows exactly what your child completed and how they scored.

**Best for:** Families needing a program children can run almost entirely themselves during the day.

---

### 2. Schoolhouse Teachers — Best Subscription for Variety
**Price:** ~$139/year for the whole family

[Schoolhouse Teachers](https://schoolhouseteachers.com/ref/119831/) gives your entire family access to hundreds of courses across all subjects for one annual fee. Children can pursue their interests independently — coding, art history, writing, and more. The low annual price makes this extraordinary value for multi-child families.

**Best for:** Families wanting a flexible library of courses children can explore independently.

---

### 3. Masterbooks — Best Structured Independent Work
**Price:** $30–$80 per subject

[Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187) is designed around a 4-day week with clear, self-contained daily lessons. Older children (5th grade and up) can read the lessons, complete review questions, and self-check against answer keys largely on their own.

**Best for:** Christian families wanting structured print curriculum older children can work through independently.

---

### 4. 7Sisters Homeschool — Best for High School
**Price:** Free–$85 per course
**Grades:** 9–12

[7Sisters Homeschool](https://www.7sistershomeschool.com/?wpam_id=52054) courses are designed for independent high school study. Their no-busywork philosophy means assignments are completable without a parent present for most work. High schoolers manage most courses themselves with parents reviewing work weekly.

**Best for:** High school families where the student is motivated to work independently.

---

## Scheduling Strategies That Work

**The Split Day Model:** Children work independently during work hours following a written schedule. You teach new concepts and review completed work in the evenings (30–60 minutes). Works well from about 3rd grade up.

**The Co-op Solution:** Many working families rely on homeschool co-ops for 1–3 days per week of structured learning while you work.

**The Weekend Intensive:** Some families concentrate direct teaching in longer blocks on weekends and leave weekdays for independent review and practice.

---

## Tools That Make It Work

[Magnetic Chore/Schedule Chart](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07DHHQXQB?tag=homeschoold00-20) — helps children track their own daily tasks independently
[Time Timer](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00000J1EW?tag=homeschoold00-20) — makes abstract time concrete for children who struggle staying on task
[Homeschool Planner](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1934386154?tag=homeschoold00-20) — for tracking progress across multiple children

---

# How to Homeschool on a Tight Budget

The average homeschool family spends $700–$1,800 per year on curriculum. But here's the truth: some of the most effective homeschool programs in existence are completely free, and a solid K–12 education can be delivered for well under $200 a year if you know where to look.

This is not about cutting corners. Price and quality have almost no correlation in homeschool curriculum.

---

## Free Curricula That Actually Work

**Easy Peasy All-in-One** — A complete, self-directed K–12 curriculum at zero cost. No trials, no upsells. Thousands of families use it as their primary program.

**Khan Academy** — The gold standard for free math from counting through AP Calculus. Also strong for science, history, and test prep.

**Ambleside Online** — A complete Charlotte Mason K–12 curriculum. Most books are public domain, available free via Project Gutenberg or your library.

**Mystery Science** — Free for homeschoolers. Complete K–5 science units using household materials.

**CK-12** — Free standards-aligned digital textbooks for middle and high school science and math.

---

## Affordable Paid Options

**[Simply Charlotte Mason](https://simplycharlottemason.com/ref/0v1y8/)** — $30–$60 for a year's worth of guidance. Most books are free at your library.

**[7Sisters Homeschool](https://www.7sistershomeschool.com/?wpam_id=52054)** — Individual courses from free to $85. A complete high school course for under $30 is exceptional value.

**[Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187)** — Complete subject sets for $30–$80 with a built-in 4-day week format.

**[Schoolhouse Teachers](https://schoolhouseteachers.com/ref/119831/)** — ~$139/year for unlimited family access to hundreds of courses. Outstanding value for multi-child families.

---

## Budget Strategies That Make a Real Difference

**Use your library aggressively.** Your library card is your most powerful homeschool tool. Charlotte Mason and literature-based curricula are built around books available free at any public library. Many libraries also offer free digital audiobooks (Libby/OverDrive), museum passes, and language learning apps.

**Buy used.** Facebook Marketplace, Homeschool Classifieds, and local co-op swap events regularly have curricula at 40–60% of retail price.

**Wait for sales.** [Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html) and [Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187) both run significant promotions in January, spring, and July. Signing up for email lists is worth it purely for the sale notifications.

**Buy only what you'll actually use.** The most common budget mistake is buying too much. Start lean and add only what you genuinely need.

---

## Sample Budget by Grade

**Kindergarten — $0:** Easy Peasy + Khan Academy Kids + library books + nature walks

**Elementary (1–5) — Under $100/year:** [All About Reading](https://www.allaboutlearningpress.net/go.php?id=3107) (~$100/level) + Khan Academy (free) + Mystery Science (free)

**Middle School — Under $150/year:** Khan Academy (free) + Ambleside Online (free) + [Schoolhouse Teachers](https://schoolhouseteachers.com/ref/119831/) ($139/family)

**High School — Under $200/year:** [7Sisters](https://www.7sistershomeschool.com/?wpam_id=52054) for language arts + Khan Academy (free) + CK-12 (free) + [Schoolhouse Teachers](https://schoolhouseteachers.com/ref/119831/) ($139/family)

---

## Essential Low-Cost Supplies

[Composition notebooks 12-pack](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B003U3WUGI?tag=homeschoold00-20) — ~$12, essential for narration and writing
[Pencils bulk pack](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B002EMQO8I?tag=homeschoold00-20) — ~$10
[Dry erase board](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07KQGVZ5G?tag=homeschoold00-20) — ~$15 for modeling lessons
[Basic school supply kit](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01N3Z0J8I?tag=homeschoold00-20) — ~$20 for pencils, rulers, scissors

---

blogPosts.content:
# How to Start Homeschooling: A Complete Beginner's Guide

Every homeschool family starts at the same place: excited, overwhelmed, and slightly terrified. The amount of information available is genuinely staggering for a new family. This guide cuts through all of it.

---

## Step 1: Know Your State's Requirements

Homeschooling is legal in all 50 states, but requirements vary enormously. Some states require virtually nothing. Others require portfolios, annual assessments, and superintendent approval. Before you do anything else, look up your state on our [State Homeschool Laws page](/legal.html).

---

## Step 2: Deschool Before You School

If your child is coming out of traditional school, most veterans recommend a period of "deschooling" before beginning formal curriculum — roughly one month per year they were in school. This is a deliberate reset where children rediscover natural curiosity without scheduled lessons. Resist the urge to start school immediately.

---

## Step 3: Figure Out Your Teaching Philosophy

**Traditional:** You prefer structured lessons, textbooks, and grades.
**Charlotte Mason:** You believe children learn best through living books, nature, and narration.
**Classical:** You want to train your child's mind through the trivium.
**Eclectic:** You want to mix and match what works.
**Interest-Led:** You trust children to direct their own learning.

Not sure? Take our [curriculum quiz](/) for personalized suggestions.

---

## Step 4: Choose a Starting Curriculum

**For structure and low prep:** [Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187) — complete subject sets with 4-day lesson plans, everything laid out for you.

**For literature-rich learning:** [Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html) — open the Instructor's Guide, follow the daily plan, read the books together. One of the easiest complete curricula to implement.

**For a gentle start:** [Simply Charlotte Mason](https://simplycharlottemason.com/ref/0v1y8/) — practical guides that help you implement CM without overwhelming theory.

**To try before investing:** Easy Peasy All-in-One (free) — complete K–12 at zero cost.

**For variety while exploring:** [Schoolhouse Teachers](https://schoolhouseteachers.com/ref/119831/) — hundreds of courses on one subscription to explore what works.

---

## Step 5: Set Up Your Space

You do not need a dedicated schoolroom. Thousands of families homeschool at the kitchen table. What you do need:

[Stackable storage drawers](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00M3XINKC?tag=homeschoold00-20) — keeps supplies organized without taking over a room
[Small magnetic whiteboard](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07KQGVZ5G?tag=homeschoold00-20) — for modeling, daily schedules, and math instruction
[Homeschool planner](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1934386154?tag=homeschoold00-20) — for organizing lessons and tracking progress

---

## Step 6: Build a Simple Daily Rhythm

**Kindergarten–2nd grade:** 1.5–2 hours of focused work
**3rd–5th grade:** 2–3 hours
**6th–8th grade:** 3–4 hours
**High school:** 4–5 hours

Structure your day with hardest subjects first (math and language arts), then lighter subjects. End on something your child enjoys.

---

## Step 7: Find Your Community

Isolation is the most commonly cited challenge of homeschooling. Counter it from the start: join a local co-op, find your state homeschool association, and connect with local Facebook groups.

---

## Step 8: Give Yourself Grace

Your first year will not be perfect. You will have bad weeks. You will question your decision. This is normal. Commit to one full year before evaluating whether homeschooling is working.

---

# How Much Does Homeschooling Cost? A Real Budget Breakdown

If you ask ten homeschool families how much they spend, you'll get ten very different answers. The cost of homeschooling is almost entirely within your control.

---

## The Average — and What Drives It

The average family spends $700–$1,800 per year on curriculum and materials. But this includes families spending $2,000 on complete packaged curricula AND families spending $50 on a couple of PDF guides while using free resources for everything else.

The single biggest cost driver is curriculum choice.

---

## Budget Breakdowns by Approach

### Fully Free — $0/year
- Easy Peasy All-in-One (K–12, free)
- Khan Academy for math and test prep (free)
- Mystery Science for elementary science (free)
- Library books for everything else

A genuinely complete education at no cost.

### Budget-Conscious Mix — $100–$300/child/year
- [All About Reading](https://www.allaboutlearningpress.net/go.php?id=3107) (~$100/level) for phonics
- Khan Academy for math (free)
- [Schoolhouse Teachers](https://schoolhouseteachers.com/ref/119831/) (~$139/family/year) for electives and variety
- Library books for history and science

### Moderate Investment — $300–$700/child/year
- [Simply Charlotte Mason](https://simplycharlottemason.com/ref/0v1y8/) guides + library books (~$60)
- [Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187) for math or science (~$80–$100)
- [Story of the World](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1945841036?tag=homeschoold00-20) for history (~$50)
- Art supplies, field trips, co-op fees (~$100–$200)

### Full Investment — $700–$1,500+/child/year
- [Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html) complete core (~$400–$600)
- Math-U-See or RightStart with manipulatives (~$150–$200)
- IEW writing program (~$100–$200)
- Co-op tuition (~$200–$600/year)

---

## Hidden Costs New Families Miss

**Consumables:** Student workbooks get written in each year — factor this in for every curriculum with consumable pages.

**Manipulatives:** Math programs like Math-U-See require physical sets ($150–$300 upfront, but last for multiple children).

**Testing:** Some states require annual assessments ($25–$60 each).

**Co-op fees:** $50–$600/year depending on your program.

**Technology:** If using online programs, factor in reliable internet and a device per child.

---

## How to Reduce Your Costs

**Buy used.** Buy previous editions or gently used sets at 40–60% of retail through Facebook groups or Homeschool Classifieds.

**Wait for sales.** [Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html) and [Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187) run significant sales in January, spring, and July–August.

**Don't buy everything at once.** Start with math and language arts. Add everything else once you know what approach works.

---

## The One Thing Worth Paying For

If your budget allows only one purchase, make it a solid math program. Math is the subject most difficult to teach well from free resources alone through algebra and beyond.

**Starter supplies that matter:**
[Composition notebooks 12-pack](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B003U3WUGI?tag=homeschoold00-20) — ~$12
[Pencils bulk pack](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B002EMQO8I?tag=homeschoold00-20) — ~$10
[Dry erase board](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07KQGVZ5G?tag=homeschoold00-20) — ~$15
[Rulers and basic math tools](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07T7M1ZJR?tag=homeschoold00-20) — ~$10

---

# What Does a Homeschool Day Actually Look Like?

When people ask about homeschooling, they often picture a child sitting at a desk for 6 hours while a parent teaches at a whiteboard. Real homeschool days are shorter, more flexible, more varied, and significantly more human than that.

---

## The First Thing to Understand: Efficiency

A one-on-one education is dramatically more efficient than a classroom. When a teacher has 25 students, they spend most of their time on classroom management and waiting. The actual instructional time a single child receives is surprisingly small.

At home, when a child understands something, you move on. When they don't, you slow down. This is why a 2–3 hour homeschool day often covers as much as a 6-hour school day.

---

## A Typical Day by Age

### Early Elementary (K–2): About 1.5–2 Hours

**8:30am** — Morning Time: 15 min of reading aloud, a poem, a song
**8:45am** — Reading/Phonics: 20 min with [All About Reading](https://www.allaboutlearningpress.net/go.php?id=3107)
**9:05am** — Free play break: 20 min
**9:25am** — Math: 20 min with manipulatives
**9:45am** — Art/Science activity: 20–30 min
**10:15am** — School done

At this age, learning to read and developing number sense is everything. The short lessons and generous play time are developmentally appropriate.

---

### Upper Elementary (3–5): About 2.5–3 Hours

**8:00am** — Morning Time: 20 min — poetry, history read-aloud, review
**8:20am** — Math: 30–40 min
**9:00am** — Language Arts: 30 min
**9:30am** — Break: 15 min
**9:45am** — History/Science: 30–40 min
**10:25am** — Independent reading: 20–30 min
**10:50am** — School done

---

### Middle School (6–8): About 3–4 Hours

**8:00am** — Independent math: 45 min
**8:45am** — Language arts: 30–40 min
**9:30am** — History: 40 min (reading + narration)
**10:10am** — Break
**10:25am** — Science: 40 min
**11:05am** — Elective or independent reading: 30 min

With [Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187), the student reads the lesson and answers review questions independently. With [Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html), assigned reading happens independently while discussions happen at dinner.

---

### High School (9–12): About 4–5 Hours

High schoolers manage their own time across 5–6 subjects per semester. [7Sisters Homeschool](https://www.7sistershomeschool.com/?wpam_id=52054) is particularly well-designed for independent study — courses can be completed without a parent present for most assignments.

---

## What Real Homeschool Days Also Include

**Read-alouds:** Even middle and high schoolers benefit from being read to.

**Nature time:** Daily outdoor time is a cornerstone of Charlotte Mason and simply good for children.

**Life skills:** Cooking, cleaning, budgeting — these appear naturally in a home-based education.

**Projects:** Long-form work that teaches skills no worksheet can replicate.

---

## The Honest Part: What a Hard Day Looks Like

Some days the math lesson takes three times as long because someone is in tears. Some days nothing formal happens at all.

Most experienced homeschoolers have a simple policy for hard days: do math and reading, then stop. The minimum is maintained, nothing is forced, and tomorrow is a fresh start.

[A good homeschool planner](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1934386154?tag=homeschoold00-20) helps you track what's been covered so you know you can afford a light day occasionally without falling behind.

---

# What Is Unschooling? Is It Right for Your Family?

Of all the homeschooling approaches, unschooling generates the most questions — and among its practitioners, the most passionate advocacy.

Mention it at a dinner party and you'll get raised eyebrows. *"You just let them do whatever they want?"*

The answer is more nuanced than that.

---

## The Core Idea

Unschooling is based on the premise that children are natural learners who, given freedom, resources, and a rich environment, will pursue knowledge with genuine curiosity and retain it far better than anything delivered through scheduled lessons.

The term was coined by educator John Holt in the 1970s. Holt observed that children learn to walk, talk, and navigate complex social worlds without formal instruction. He argued the same natural drive applies to academic subjects when children are genuinely interested and not coerced.

In practice, unschooling means:
- No mandatory curriculum or lessons
- Child-directed learning based on interests
- Parents as facilitators and resource providers, not teachers
- Life as the curriculum — cooking, travel, conversations, projects, community involvement

---

## What Unschooling Is NOT

**It is not neglect.** Unschooling parents are typically more engaged with their children's learning, not less. They provide resources, answer questions, and facilitate experiences constantly.

**It is not "no rules."** Unschooling applies to education, not to family life. Household rules, boundaries, and expectations remain.

**It does not guarantee academic gaps.** Unschooled children passionate about a subject often develop expertise that far exceeds schooled peers.

---

## The Case For Unschooling

When children pursue learning because they genuinely want to know something, they retain it and pursue it further. Intrinsic motivation produces deeper learning than compelled study.

There's also a compelling argument about self-direction. Children who learn to follow their own curiosity become adults who can identify problems worth solving and pursue them independently.

---

## The Honest Concerns

**The math problem.** Mathematics is the one subject that doesn't emerge reliably from interest-led learning. Most unschooling families who are honest about this provide some structured math instruction while leaving everything else unstructured.

**College access.** Unschooled students do gain college admission, but it requires more documentation, more test preparation, and more advocacy.

**Parent temperament.** You must genuinely trust your child's learning process and resist anxiety about "falling behind." This is harder than it sounds.

---

## Is It Right for Your Family?

Unschooling works well if you genuinely trust children as natural learners, can maintain a rich home environment, are in a state with relaxed laws, and your child is naturally curious and self-motivated.

It may not fit if you need external structure and validation, your child has learning differences benefiting from structured intervention, or you have immediate college ambitions requiring a traditional transcript.

---

## Essential Reading

[How Children Learn by John Holt](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0738208702?tag=homeschoold00-20) — the foundational text
[Free to Learn by Peter Gray](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0465084990?tag=homeschoold00-20) — the most compelling modern case for unschooling
[Unschooling Rules by Clark Aldrich](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1608321169?tag=homeschoold00-20) — practical principles

---

## A Middle Path

Many families drawn to unschooling's principles but finding pure unschooling too unstructured adopt "relaxed homeschooling" — light structure for math and reading, combined with abundant freedom for everything else. [Simply Charlotte Mason](https://simplycharlottemason.com/ref/0v1y8/) offers a gentle framework that honors child-led learning without abandoning all structure.

---

blogPosts.content:
# What Is the Charlotte Mason Method? Everything You Need to Know

Charlotte Mason (1842–1923) was a British educator who spent her career arguing that children deserve a richer, more humane education than the rote memorization and dry textbooks of her era. Her ideas are as relevant today as they were in Victorian England.

---

## The Foundation: "Children Are Born Persons"

Mason's most fundamental principle: children are not blank slates to be filled, but full human beings with inherent dignity, curiosity, and the capacity for genuine ideas.

This has practical consequences. If a child is a person, they deserve real books — not dumbed-down summaries. They deserve to encounter great ideas — not simplified facts. They deserve to be trusted to think, not just to repeat.

---

## The Three Instruments

Mason described education as depending on three things: **Atmosphere, Discipline, and Life.**

**Atmosphere** — the environment of the home, the values, the intellectual life of the family.
**Discipline** — the formation of good habits built slowly and consistently over time.
**Life** — the curriculum itself: education as a life, not preparation for life.

---

## The Key Practices

### Living Books
A living book is written by someone who genuinely loves their subject and writes about it with passion. It makes a subject come alive. The opposite — what Mason called "twaddle" — is dumbed-down, lifeless content that talks down to children. Replace textbooks with real literature wherever possible.

### Narration
Instead of tests and worksheets, ask a child to tell back what they just read in their own words. If a child can narrate a passage well, they understood it. This is Charlotte Mason's primary assessment tool.

### Short Lessons
15–20 minute lesson blocks for young children, varied throughout the day. Attention is finite. Forcing a tired child past their capacity produces nothing but resentment.

### Nature Study
Regular time outdoors for direct observation of the natural world — not structured science, just being in nature and noticing.

### Copywork and Dictation
Children copy passages from good literature, absorbing spelling, punctuation, and style through imitation — rather than through grammar drills.

### Picture Study and Composer Study
Each term, study one artist and one composer in depth — looking carefully at their works and learning about their lives.

---

## Charlotte Mason Curricula

**[Simply Charlotte Mason](https://simplycharlottemason.com/ref/0v1y8/)** — The most practical entry point. Sonya Shafer's guides explain the methods clearly and provide concrete schedules and book lists. Highly recommended for families new to CM.

**Ambleside Online** — The most rigorous free CM curriculum. Complete year-by-year schedules, most books available free online.

**[Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html)** — Literature-rich, read-aloud centered, deeply CM-influenced. One of the most accessible entry points.

**[Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187)** — CM-influenced traditional curriculum. More structured but shares the emphasis on real books.

---

## Essential Reading

[Home Education by Charlotte Mason (Volume 1)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1528716817?tag=homeschoold00-20) — start here
[A Charlotte Mason Companion by Karen Andreola](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1889209023?tag=homeschoold00-20) — most accessible modern introduction
[The Brave Learner by Julie Bogart](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0143133329?tag=homeschoold00-20) — a modern CM-influenced take

---

## Is Charlotte Mason Right for You?

It works beautifully if you love books and reading aloud, want school to feel gentle and relationship-centered, and are comfortable with less measurable forms of assessment.

It may not fit if you or your child need highly structured days, your child has significant reading difficulties, or you need standardized grades for documentation.

The good news: CM principles can be incorporated partially. Many families use CM for history and literature while using traditional approaches for math and language arts.

---

blogPosts.content:
# What Is a Unit Study? How to Use Them in Your Homeschool

A unit study is a deep, extended exploration of a single topic that weaves multiple subjects together around one theme. Instead of studying history, science, and language arts separately, a unit study integrates them all through one central subject.

Study ancient Egypt for six weeks and you're doing history, science (mummification, agriculture, astronomy), geography, writing, art, and math — all naturally connected. One topic, six subjects.

---

## Why Unit Studies Work

**Deep beats shallow.** The traditional approach of covering a new chapter every week produces shallow familiarity with dozens of topics. Six weeks on ancient Egypt produces knowledge children actually remember.

**Integration creates meaning.** When a child reads about how the Nile floods created fertile farmland (science) while also reading about Egyptian farmers in a historical novel (language arts), the concepts reinforce each other.

**Multi-age learning.** A 7-year-old and a 12-year-old can study ancient Egypt together — reading different books, completing different activities, but learning the same history. One of unit studies' most practical advantages for multi-child families.

**Engagement.** Children who shut down during textbook instruction often come alive with unit studies. The variety of activities — projects, experiments, art, cooking, field trips — keeps different learners engaged.

---

## The Limitation: Potential Gaps

Unit studies can create uneven coverage. A family that loves American history might study the Civil War three times and never cover medieval Europe. The standard advice: use unit studies for history and science where coverage can be flexible, and maintain systematic curricula for math and language arts where skill sequences matter.

---

## Best Unit Study Curricula

### Five in a Row — Best for Young Children
**Ages:** Pre-K through 2nd grade
**Cost:** ~$80–$150 per volume

Each unit is built around a classic picture book read together for five days. From one story you pull language arts, math, science, social studies, and art.

[Mike Mulligan and His Steam Shovel](https://www.amazon.com/dp/039516717X?tag=homeschoold00-20) — a classic FIAR title
[Make Way for Ducklings](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0140564381?tag=homeschoold00-20) — another beloved FIAR book
[The Story About Ping](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0448425963?tag=homeschoold00-20) — perfect for a geography unit

### Story of the World — Best History Spine
**Ages:** K–8

Not strictly a unit study, but functions as a perfect spine. Each chapter covers a historical period; families build activities, living books, crafts, and projects around each chapter.

[Story of the World Volume 1: Ancient Times](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1945841036?tag=homeschoold00-20) — the most popular starting point.

### Sonlight — Best Literature-Based Approach
[Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html) integrates history, literature, and language arts around historical time periods — essentially curated, literature-rich unit studies for each grade level.

### Simply Charlotte Mason — Best for Building Your Own
[Simply Charlotte Mason](https://simplycharlottemason.com/ref/0v1y8/) provides the framework for building your own unit studies around living books and CM principles.

---

## How to Build a Simple Unit Study

1. **Choose a topic** your child is genuinely interested in
2. **Find a spine** — a strong narrative non-fiction book or historical novel
3. **Map subjects to the topic:** history, science, language arts, art, math connections
4. **Gather resources:** library books, documentaries, field trips, craft supplies
5. **Plan for 3–6 weeks** at your comfortable pace

[The Ultimate Unit Study Planner](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0974934801?tag=homeschoold00-20) — helpful for organizing your units
[Unit study supply kit](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01N3Z0J8I?tag=homeschoold00-20) — poster board, markers, index cards for project work

---

## Who Are Unit Studies Best For?

Best for: multi-age families, kinesthetic and visual learners, children with intense interests, families who want memorable school experiences.

Less ideal as a primary approach for: children needing highly sequential instruction, families needing standardized coverage for assessments, or parents who find DIY planning overwhelming.

---

blogPosts.content:
# Homeschool Curriculum Sales: When to Buy and Where to Find Deals

Most major publishers run sales at specific, predictable times of year. Knowing those windows can save you hundreds of dollars on the same programs you were going to buy anyway.

---

## The Annual Sales Calendar

### January–February: New Year Sales
Good for: re-evaluating mid-year, planning ahead, experimenting with a new curriculum at a discount.

Watch for: [Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html) often runs significant January promotions. [Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187) and [Simply Charlotte Mason](https://simplycharlottemason.com/ref/0v1y8/) also typically offer new year deals.

### March–May: Curriculum Fair Season
Spring is when most major homeschool curriculum fairs happen. Publishers offer their best pricing to drive conference traffic, and many offer "conference pricing" on their websites simultaneously. This is historically one of the best times for deals on premium curricula.

### July–August: Back to School — Biggest Sales of the Year
The largest sales of the homeschool year. If you know what you want for next year, **wait until mid-July to buy** whenever possible.

Typically on sale:
- [Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html) — bundle discounts
- [Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187) — package deals and free shipping
- [All About Learning Press](https://www.allaboutlearningpress.net/go.php?id=3107) — seasonal promotions
- [Simply Charlotte Mason](https://simplycharlottemason.com/ref/0v1y8/) — annual sale
- [Schoolhouse Teachers](https://schoolhouseteachers.com/ref/119831/) — subscription deals
- [7Sisters Homeschool](https://www.7sistershomeschool.com/?wpam_id=52054) — course bundles
- [Veritas Press](https://veritaspress.referralrock.com/l/VANESSAHEE37/) — curriculum packages

### November–December: Black Friday and Cyber Monday
Discounts range from 20–40%. Digital products often go deeper. Good for buying next year's curriculum early or stocking up on consumables.

---

## Where to Find the Best Deals

**Publisher email lists** — Signing up for any publisher you're interested in is the single most effective way to catch sales. Most announce sales to subscribers first. Sign up for [Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html), [Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187), [Simply Charlotte Mason](https://simplycharlottemason.com/ref/0v1y8/), [7Sisters](https://www.7sistershomeschool.com/?wpam_id=52054), [Schoolhouse Teachers](https://schoolhouseteachers.com/ref/119831/), and [All About Learning Press](https://www.allaboutlearningpress.net/go.php?id=3107).

**Homeschool Buyers Co-op** — Negotiates group discounts and passes savings directly to members. Free to join and consistently underused.

**Facebook homeschool groups** — Groups dedicated to curriculum deals post sales in real time. Worth joining even if you're not otherwise active on Facebook.

**Curriculum fairs** — In-person fairs routinely offer the best pricing of the year. You also get to see materials in person before buying.

**Used curriculum markets** — Homeschool Classifieds, local Facebook Marketplace, and co-op swap days regularly have curricula at 40–60% of retail.

---

## Smart Buying Strategies

**Buy last year's edition.** Most curriculum updates are minor. A two-year-old history or science curriculum is usually identical in content, and used copies can be found for 40–60% of retail.

**Buy the Teacher's Guide new, consumables as needed.** Teacher's guides last for multiple children. Only student workbooks need replacing annually.

**Time your purchases.** If you're deciding between curricula in June, wait until the July sales. You'll often save 20–30% on the same product.

**Don't hoard.** Buying curriculum "just in case" leads to unused materials gathering dust. Buy what you'll use in the next 12 months.

---

## Publisher-Specific Tips

**[Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html)** — Their "Build Your Package" tool lets you customize orders. Sales apply to packages, so buying components together during a sale beats buying individually at full price.

**[Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187)** — Look for their "Complete Curriculum" bundles which offer significant savings over individual titles.

**[All About Learning Press](https://www.allaboutlearningpress.net/go.php?id=3107)** — Their AAR + AAS bundles offer meaningful savings. Watch for their semi-annual sales.

**[7Sisters](https://www.7sistershomeschool.com/?wpam_id=52054)** — Already very affordable at $15–$30 per course. Their year-of-English bundles offer the best per-course value.

**[Veritas Press](https://veritaspress.referralrock.com/l/VANESSAHEE37/)** — Self-paced digital courses go on sale periodically. Bundle pricing for multiple courses is significantly better than course-by-course.

**[Schoolhouse Teachers](https://schoolhouseteachers.com/ref/119831/)** — Annual subscription offers the best value. Watch for Black Friday when annual prices often drop significantly.

---

blogPosts.title: Homeschool Curriculum Fair Guide: What to Buy, What to Skip
blogPosts.excerpt: Curriculum fairs are exciting, overwhelming, and full of opportunities to spend money you don't need to spend. Here's how to go in prepared, leave with what…
blogPosts.content:
# Homeschool Curriculum Fair Guide: What to Buy, What to Skip

The first time you walk into a large homeschool curriculum fair, the experience is almost overwhelming. Hundreds of vendor booths, thousands of products, and the sinking feeling that your children are receiving an inferior education because you don't own all of it.

That feeling is the enemy. Here's how to walk in with a plan and leave with what you actually need.

---

## Before You Go: Do Your Research

**Know what subjects you're covering next year.** Make a simple list with your approach preferences and rough budget for each subject.

**Use our tools first.** Spend 20 minutes on our [curriculum comparison tool](/) and take our [quiz](/). Walk in knowing your top 2–3 options for each subject. This prevents expensive impulse purchases.

**Request free samples in advance.** [Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html), [Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187), [Simply Charlotte Mason](https://simplycharlottemason.com/ref/0v1y8/), and [All About Learning Press](https://www.allaboutlearningpress.net/go.php?id=3107) all offer downloadable samples. Do this before the fair, not during.

**Set a hard budget.** Write it down and keep it with you. A number in your head is not as effective as a number on paper.

---

## What Curriculum Fairs Are Good For

**Seeing materials in person.** This is the irreplaceable value of a fair. You can hold the books, flip through teacher's guides, feel the paper quality, and see whether the design appeals to your child. Many curricula that look great online disappoint in person — and vice versa.

**Asking real questions.** Publisher representatives are usually knowledgeable. This is your chance to ask: "Does this work for a child who resists writing?" "What's the daily time commitment?"

**Fair-only pricing.** Most publishers offer their best prices of the year at fairs. If you've already researched and know what you want, this is a legitimate reason to wait and buy in person.

---

## What to Bring

[A sturdy tote bag](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01LWZGFGQ?tag=homeschoold00-20) — for carrying catalogs and samples throughout the day
A notebook or phone for notes on booth numbers you want to return to
Comfortable shoes — large fairs cover significant floor space
Snacks and water — food at convention centers is expensive
**Your budget in writing**

---

## The Three-Pass Strategy

**First pass: Collect, don't buy.** Walk every aisle. Pick up catalogs and free samples. Take photos. Write down booth numbers. Do not buy anything.

**Second pass: Evaluate.** Return to booths that interested you. Ask your specific questions. Compare actual products to your research.

**Third pass: Buy.** Now make your purchases with your budget in hand and your list in front of you.

This strategy prevents the most common mistake: buying the first exciting thing before you've seen everything.

---

## What to Buy at a Fair

- Materials you've already researched and know you want — fair pricing justifies buying now
- Consumables you'll need anyway (student workbooks, composition notebooks)
- Sample packs from publishers you're genuinely considering
- Anything you've looked through carefully and that fits your budget

---

## What to Skip

- Anything you haven't researched — an enthusiastic sales pitch is not research
- Curricula for subjects that are already working well
- "Just in case" purchases — if you don't have a specific plan for it, don't buy it
- Anything that significantly exceeds your budget
- Duplicate subjects — two complete history spines or three phonics programs help no one

---

## Best Curricula to Look For at Fairs

- **[Sonlight](https://affiliate.sonlight.com/242.html)** — Seeing the books in person makes a real difference for this investment
- **[Masterbooks](https://www.masterbooks.com/?acc=ef894c78653fadf33240b9a72567b187)** — Good fair bundle deals and friendly representatives
- **[All About Learning Press](https://www.allaboutlearningpress.net/go.php?id=3107)** — Try the letter tiles and manipulatives in person
- **[Simply Charlotte Mason](https://simplycharlottemason.com/ref/0v1y8/)** — Great for talking through the CM approach with knowledgeable staff
- **[Veritas Press](https://veritaspress.referralrock.com/l/VANESSAHEE37/)** — Complex curriculum worth seeing before buying
- **[7Sisters Homeschool](https://www.7sistershomeschool.com/?wpam_id=52054)** — Often present at major conventions

---

## After the Fair

**Give yourself 48 hours before buying anything you're unsure about.** If you still want it after two days, buy it. If you've forgotten about it, you didn't need it.

**Sell what doesn't work quickly.** If something you bought turns out to be wrong for your family, resell it fast through Homeschool Classifieds or local Facebook groups. The faster you resell, the more you recover.

---
